lecture_context.lecture_title: Lecture 9 - Attention, Final Projects and LLM Intro

unit.name: Machine Translation Evaluation with BLEU
unit.description: Introduces automatic evaluation for machine translation, explains BLEU, and situates neural MT historically against phrase-based and syntax-based systems.
unit.summary: The segment explains why machine translation needs automatic evaluation and why BLEU became the dominant metric despite known flaws. BLEU scores candidate translations by overlap with one or more human reference translations using n-grams, while also adding a brevity penalty to discourage systems from translating only easy parts [ts=207s] [ts=451s]. The lecturer stresses that translation has many valid outputs, so BLEU can underrate good translations and still award partial credit to bad ones if local words match [ts=355s] [ts=417s]. He then uses BLEU trends to contrast stalled phrase-based and syntax-based MT with the steep improvement of neural MT after 2014–2016, noting modern systems can reach much higher BLEU ranges than earlier systems [ts=520s] [ts=689s].

key_points.text: BLEU evaluates a system translation by n-gram overlap against human reference translations.
BLEU traditionally uses up to 4-gram matches, though the cutoff is a design choice rather than a law.
BLEU includes a brevity penalty so systems cannot score highly by translating only easy fragments.
Translation has many valid outputs, so BLEU can miss good paraphrastic translations.
Neural machine translation rapidly surpassed phrase-based and syntax-based MT after 2015–2016.

The lecturer stresses that translation has many valid outputs, so BLEU can underrate good translations and still award partial credit to bad ones if local words match [ts=355s] [ts=417s]. He then uses BLEU trends to contrast stalled phrase-based and syntax-based MT with the steep improvement of neural MT after 2014–2016, noting modern systems can reach much higher BLEU ranges than earlier systems [ts=520s] [ts=689s].

kp_catalog.name: BLEU and overlap-based text evaluation
kp_catalog.description: Automatic MT evaluation based on matching candidate n-grams against one or more reference translations, with a brevity penalty. Reference-based overlap metrics compare generated text to human references using lexical or n-gram overlap.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because bLEU and overlap-based text evaluation clarifies automatic MT evaluation based on matching candidate n-grams against one or more reference translations, with a brevity penalty, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Limitations of reference-based evaluation
kp_catalog.description: BLEU is useful but imperfect because translation is many-to-many and local string overlap does not fully capture meaning or grammatical role. Even a decent scoring metric can fail if the human references used as gold outputs are weak or noisy.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because limitations of reference-based evaluation clarifies bLEU is useful but imperfect because translation is many-to-many and local string overlap does not fully capture meaning or grammatical role, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices though importance labels split between high and medium.
